common.footerTagline: Independent consultant and trainer for software engineering teams.

home.heroTitle: I help software engineering teams ship faster with consistent engineering practices.
home.heroSubtitle: I work with mid–senior teams on DevOps, architecture, and everyday practices that reduce friction instead of adding process overhead.
home.heroEyebrow: Classroom training and consulting for software engineering teams.
home.problemsTitle: Typical problems I see in engineering teams
problems: Delivery slows down as the codebase grows; releases feel risky and fragile.
Different teams (or even individuals) follow incompatible conventions and tooling.
Architecture decisions are undocumented; new engineers have to reverse-engineer intent from code.
Infrastructure and deployment scripts are tribal knowledge instead of shared practice.
People "know" good practices in theory, but they don’t apply them consistently under pressure.
focusOn: Safer changes: tests and pipelines that catch problems before production instead of after.
Faster delivery: fewer bottlenecks, clearer handoffs, and less rework from inconsistent practices.
Visible architecture: boundaries and decisions documented so the next person can pick them up.
Repeatable practices: versioned infrastructure and shared conventions instead of tribal knowledge.
Confidence in refactoring: modular design and dependency rules that make change predictable.
Delivery feedback you can trust: CI/CD that gives fast, reliable signals so releases stop feeling risky.
offerings: Engineering practices: code review, branching, modular design, and habits that keep codebases maintainable.
DevOps fundamentals: from CI pipelines to pragmatic monitoring, focused on what your team can actually adopt.
Architecture decision records (ADR): how to capture decisions so they survive beyond the current project.
C4 diagrams: lightweight visual models that help teams reason about systems together.
Clean architecture: structuring systems so that business rules stay decoupled from frameworks and infrastructure.
offeringsEngineering: Code review as a consistency and learning lever, not a gate.
Branching and integration strategies that keep main releasable and reduce merge pain.
Modular design and boundaries that keep the codebase navigable as it grows.
Definition of done and quality gates that teams can actually sustain under pressure.
offeringsDevOps: CI pipelines that give fast, reliable feedback and fail in a way that blocks broken builds.
Trunk-based development vs. long-lived branches: trade-offs and when each fits.
Environments and deployment practices that support safe, repeatable releases.
Pragmatic monitoring and observability so the team can detect and diagnose issues.
offeringsArchitecture: ADRs: when to write them, what to capture, and how to keep them lightweight and useful.
C4 diagrams: a shared visual language to reason about systems at different levels.
Clean architecture: dependency rules and boundaries that keep business logic decoupled from frameworks and infrastructure.
Structuring systems so that future changes don't require rewrites or heroics.
whyDifferent: Senior-led: you work directly with me, not with a junior trainer reading from slides.
Hands-on: we work through real examples, code, and architecture from your world.
Opinionated: I share what tends to work in practice, not just what looks nice in a slide deck.
Context-aware: formats and exercises are adapted to your team’s maturity and constraints.
home.practiceIntro: Work is done on your codebase, pipelines, and architecture—not generic examples. Typical formats include:
home.practiceCardTitle: How this usually looks in practice
practiceExamples: Architecture reviews: walking through your system, identifying boundaries and coupling, and agreeing next steps.
ADR sessions: capturing decisions in a lightweight format so they survive beyond the current team.
CI/CD redesign: reviewing your pipeline and deployment flow, then designing changes for faster, safer feedback.
Refactoring workshops: guided work on real modules to introduce or tighten boundaries and dependency rules.
home.talksIntro: To get a sense of my approach, here are a few conference talks and sessions:
home.talk1Description: Conference session on Architecture Decision Records in practice
home.talk2Title: Continuous failure – Why do we make our lives hard?
home.talk2Description: On the cost of missing ADRs and documented decisions.
home.moreSessionsBody: See upcoming events and full session history on my public Sessionize profile.
home.engagementModelsIntro: Formats are adapted to your goals and team maturity. Common options:
engagementModels: 1–2 day focused workshops: sharpen one or two areas (e.g. ADRs and C4, or CI/CD and safe release) with an experienced team.
Short diagnostic engagements: review architecture and practices, identify high-impact improvements, and hand over a clear summary and next steps.
Ongoing advisory: periodic sessions to support decision-making, rollout of new practices, or course-correction as you change.
Longer-term partnership: multi-phase work when you want sustained support for a broader transformation, with scope and rhythm agreed up front.

training.pageTitle: Classroom training for experienced engineering teams
training.intro: I run focused, classroom-style workshops for software engineering teams that already deliver value but want to do it more consistently and with less friction.
format: Small to mid-sized groups where active participation is expected.
Mix of short theory blocks, code and architecture exercises, and guided discussions.
Optionally tailored around your existing codebase, CI setup, and architecture challenges.
forWho: Mid–senior software engineers who already ship to production.
Tech leads and engineering managers responsible for standards and delivery.
Teams who feel that "everyone does things slightly differently" and want a shared baseline.
notFor: Absolute beginners or bootcamp-style introductions to programming.
Teams looking for generic motivational talks without concrete follow-through.
Situations where there is no intent to change how the team actually works day-to-day.
topics: Engineering practices: code review, branching strategies, definition of done, and practical quality gates.
DevOps practices: CI/CD pipelines, trunk-based vs. GitFlow in practice, and environments that support safe change.
ADRs: when to write them, what to capture, and how to keep them lightweight but useful.
C4 diagrams: using a shared visual language to reason about systems at multiple levels.
Clean architecture: boundaries, dependency rules, and patterns that keep systems adaptable.
engagement: 1-day intensive: sharpening one or two focus areas for an experienced team.
2-day workshop: combining architectural topics (ADRs, C4, clean architecture) with delivery practices.
3-day deep dive: training plus guided work on your own architecture and pipelines.
outcomes: A shared vocabulary for architecture and practices across your team.
Clearer, documented decisions instead of implicit, person-dependent knowledge.
More predictable delivery because practices are consistent, not reinvented per feature.
Concrete next steps tailored to your context, not a generic checklist.
training.b2cCardBody: Looking for an individual track? Two-day, in-person Budapest workshop: technical decisions, C4, and lightweight ADRs—for developers moving past ticket-only work.
training.ctaNote: For training requests, a short description of your team, stack, and current challenges is more useful than a long RFP.

trainingB2cAds.pageTitle: Workshop Budapest — move beyond ticket execution
trainingB2cAds.eyebrow: 2 days · Budapest · June 11-12 (Thu-Fri) · small group · in person only
trainingB2cAds.heroTitle: Become the developer who can reason at system level.
trainingB2cAds.heroBody: Two intense, hands-on days on technical decisions, C4, and lightweight ADRs. The goal is simple: return Monday with sharper clarity, faster reasoning, and stronger trade-off communication.
trainingB2cAds.heroSub: Bring your own system or a real decision knot from work, and we build from that.
proofPoints: Not a passive talk: you work through your own decision situations.
Small in-person group with feedback, not just slides.
C4 + ADR together: diagrams, rationale, and communication in one narrative.
You leave with usable artifacts, not just motivation.
trainingB2cAds.outcomesTitle: What you take back by Monday morning
outcomes: The “Reality Check” decision checklist you can use in your next design meeting.
Context and Container C4 views for your own system, ready to paste into docs.
A practical ADR draft pattern you can reuse with your team.
A 15-minute architecture story routine for stakeholder conversations.
trainingB2cAds.instructorBody1: Krisztián is a software architect and consultant with over a decade of hands-on experience in designing, building, and evolving complex software systems. After years in senior engineering leadership roles, he founded his own consulting company, where he helps organizations modernize their architecture, accelerate delivery, and build sustainable engineering practices.

instructorBody2Parts.betweenKrisztianAndCommunity: is also the founder of the
instructorBody2Parts.afterCommunity: , creating a supportive environment where individuals can thrive, share their knowledge, and collectively contribute to the advancement of technology.

trainingB2cAds.ctaSecondaryTitle: If this is relevant now, don't postpone it
trainingB2cAds.ctaSecondaryBody: Seats are limited: 99,000 HUF until May 17, then 119,000 HUF. Date: June 11-12 (Thu-Fri).
trainingB2cAds.checkoutNote: After registration, you are redirected to a secure Stripe checkout page. You will receive an immediate email confirmation about successful payment and the next steps.

slots.title: Introduction
slots.body: Short intros and context. You walk through the workshop arc: from a problem to a decision, then to documented change.

slots.title: Structuring decisions
slots.body: You learn to state a problem clearly, surface options, and decide along trade-offs. You run through one shared example together.

slots.title: Your own decision situations
slots.body: You work on your own problems. The goal is that everyone leaves with one concrete, explicit decision.

slots.title: ADR fundamentals
slots.body: How a decision becomes short, readable documentation. What to focus on—and what is just noise.

slots.title: Writing ADRs
slots.body: You turn morning decisions into ADRs. This is where it shows how clear the decision really was.

slots.title: Review and refinement
slots.body: You read each other's ADRs, ask questions, and tighten wording so others can follow it.

slots.title: Closing
slots.body: You recap the day and how decisions shifted while writing them down.

slots.title: Warm-up
slots.body: Brief bridge from day 1: you have decisions and ADRs—now you look at their impact at system level.

slots.title: Understanding the system
slots.body: How to look at a system as the outcome of decisions. What belongs in the picture, what does not, and what makes it legible for others.

slots.title: C4 essentials
slots.body: The core of the C4 model and how to present the system at different levels.

slots.title: Shared modeling
slots.body: On one example you sketch the system together, focusing on architecture that is easy to communicate.

slots.title: Visualizing your own system
slots.body: You draw your own systems. This is where “communicable architecture” becomes concrete.

slots.title: Presentations and feedback
slots.body: You show diagrams to each other and give feedback on clarity and usefulness.

slots.title: Connecting the full flow
slots.body: You tie it together: problem, decision, ADR as change, and the current system state in C4.

trainingB2cAds.programFooterCtaBody: If the schedule fits, book your seat—we follow up by email with practical details.
trainingB2cAds.faqCancellationLead: In short, if you cannot attend:
faqCancellationPoints: More than 10 days before the event: 100% refund.
Between 7 and 10 days before the event: 50% refund.
Within 7 days or in case of no-show: no refund.
Your ticket is transferable, and late arrival does not qualify for partial refund.

faqItems.question: Who is this workshop for?
paragraphs: This workshop is for developers who want to think at system level—not only ship code.
If you have worked on real production systems and want to make better decisions around architecture, trade-offs, and complexity, it is for you.

faqItems.question: Do I need prior experience?
paragraphs: Yes—basic professional development experience is required.
It is not for absolute beginners, but you do not need to be senior. What matters is that you have faced real problems, not only tutorials.

faqItems.question: How many people can attend?
paragraphs: We keep the group small so you can ask questions and discuss—not only listen passively.

faqItems.question: Where and when is it?
paragraphs: In person in Budapest (District XIII). Exact venue and schedule are sent after you register.

faqItems.question: What happens after payment?
paragraphs: Right after payment you receive a short questionnaire so we can tailor the content even better.
If anything changes, we will notify you via email.

faqItems.question: Can I get an invoice for my company?
paragraphs: Yes. During checkout you can enter the required billing details, and we will issue the invoice accordingly.

faqItems.question: What if the workshop is cancelled?
paragraphs: If we do not reach the minimum headcount, we reschedule the workshop or refund the full amount.

faqItems.question: Will there be a recording?
paragraphs: No. This is an interactive, in-person workshop—not a lecture.
That interaction is where most of the value comes from.

faqItems.question: What will I learn in practice?
paragraphs: We work through real examples: system design decisions, understanding trade-offs, typical pitfalls and how to avoid them.
It is not a theory-only talk—it trains how you think.

trainingB2cTerms.intro: I want the workshop to be a high-quality and inspiring experience for every participant. To keep the collaboration predictable for everyone, the following rules apply to your registration.

registration.title: 1. Registration and payment
points: Your registration becomes final after you submit the registration form and pay the participation fee.
Seats are secured in the order payments are received. Until the fee arrives, I cannot guarantee your seat.

viability.title: 2. Workshop viability
viability.intro: To provide the best possible quality, the workshop requires at least 6 participants.
viability.decisionDeadlineValue: No later than 7 calendar days before the workshop starts, I decide whether the event goes ahead based on the participant count, and I also share the exact venue at that time.
ifCancelledOptions: In this case, you have two options: either I refund 100% of the paid amount within 3 business days,
or your registration can be moved to the next announced date.

participantCancellation.title: 3. Cancellation terms by the participant
participantCancellation.intro: I understand that plans can change, but due to venue and preparation costs I also need a clear framework.

bands.window: More than 10 days before the event
Between 7 and 10 days before the event

participantCancellation.transferNote: Important: Your ticket can be transferred at any time. If you cannot attend, you may send someone else in your place. In this case, please email the new participant's name and contact details.

noShowAndLate.title: 4. No-show and late arrival
points: No-show: If you do not attend the workshop and did not notify me in advance (within the deadlines above), I cannot refund the participation fee, and it cannot be applied to another date.

Late arrival: The workshop starts exactly at the announced time. Please arrive 10-15 minutes earlier. In case of delay, I cannot repeat already covered content to avoid disrupting the group's progress. No partial refund is provided for time missed due to lateness.

conductAndLiability.title: 5. Code of conduct and responsibility
points: All participants attend the workshop at their own responsibility.

If a participant's behavior (for example aggression, intoxication, or continuous disruption) blocks the professional work or disturbs other participants, the organizer has the right to remove that participant from the event. In this case, no refund is provided.

mediaConsent.title: 6. Photo and video
points: Photos or short videos may be recorded during the event for documentation and marketing purposes.
By registering, you consent to the use of these recordings. If you do not want to appear in photos or videos, please let me know before the workshop starts.

forceMajeure.title: 7. Force majeure and other terms
points: If the workshop is canceled due to my side (for example illness), the full participation fee is refunded, or you can use it for a later date (your choice).
Each participant attends the workshop at their own responsibility.
The organizer reserves the right to change the venue (to a similar category and accessibility), and participants will be informed by email.

contact.title: Have a question?
contact.intro: Feel free to contact me at:

signupPopup.title: Don’t just churn tickets—level up to systems thinking.
signupPopup.introChecklist: Enter your email address and I’ll send you my “Architect’s Reality Check” checklist right away. These are the five questions I ask in every design phase to avoid expensive, painful technology dead ends.
signupPopup.introWaitlist: You’ll also join the waitlist: as soon as new Budapest dates are set, you’ll get a notification. If you reply in a few sentences about your own project, I can tailor the workshop examples to you—so you get real solutions, not just theory.
signupPopup.submitCta: Send me the checklist and add me to the waitlist!
signupPopup.successMessage: Thanks! You’ll get the checklist shortly, and you’re on the waitlist too.

workshop.onSiteHighlight: Primary goal: learn to make strong architecture decisions as a team
workshop.title: ADRs and requirement clarity – deliver what the customer actually needs
workshop.subtitle: ADRs are not the end goal here; they are a byproduct. The core outcome is a team that can make clear, aligned architecture decisions together.

day1.title: Day 1 – How decisions are made (and broken)
day1.goal: Goal: learn how to make strong architecture decisions as a team; ADR documentation is a consequence, not the objective.

sessions.title: The problem of invisible decisions
sessions.goal: Build shared language around why decision rationale disappears.
points: "Who decided this?" as the classic end-of-project question.
Typical symptoms: "It has always been this way", "Previous team decision", "We don't touch this anymore".
ADR as memory, conflict prevention, and onboarding accelerator.
sessions.exercise: Bring one real decision from your project that nobody remembers clearly anymore.

sessions.title: What an ADR really is (and what it is not)
sessions.goal: Practice the minimal structure of a useful ADR.
points: ADR is not a design doc, not a ticket comment, and not post-factum justification.
Core anatomy: Context, Decision, Options considered, Consequences.
Depth levels: micro-ADR, normal ADR, architectural line in the sand.
sessions.exercise: Write 3 ADR variants with different depth for the same decision.

sessions.title: When to write ADRs, and when not to
sessions.goal: Define practical boundaries so ADRs stay useful.
points: Decision matrix: reversible/irreversible, local/system-wide, cheap/expensive to change.
YAGNI vs ADR: when to document up front and when later.
Anti-patterns: ADR for everything, ADR never.
sessions.exercise: Classify backlog items: ADR needed / not needed / later.

sessions.title: Ownership and decision rights
sessions.goal: Define ownership and approval flow for ADRs.
points: Who can write ADRs, who approves, and what happens without consensus.
ADR as a lightweight decision contract.
sessions.exercise: Draw the flow: idea -> discussion -> ADR -> implementation -> review.

day2.title: Day 2 – ADRs in a living system
day2.goal: Goal: embed shared decision-making into daily work, with documentation as a practical byproduct.

sessions.title: Building systems with ADRs
sessions.goal: Practice under time pressure with real trade-offs.
points: Project choices: monorepo vs multirepo, sync vs async, database, auth.
Every major decision gets an ADR under time pressure.
Learn what truly slows down and what speeds up delivery.

sessions.title: ADR lifecycle
sessions.goal: Learn to keep ADRs current and useful over time.
points: Draft -> Accepted -> Superseded -> Deprecated.
How to avoid building an ADR graveyard.
Superseding ADRs as evolution, not failure.
sessions.exercise: Supersede an older ADR and write a new one.

sessions.title: ADR and delivery flow
points: Connection to tickets, PRs, and releases.
Minimum integration: ADR link in PR, ADR ID in commit.
When ADR should not be a delivery gate.

sessions.title: AI, agents, and ADR
sessions.goal: Separate useful and harmful AI use around ADRs.
points: ADR as prompt input and institutional memory for agents.
Useful use-cases: decision summarization and impact analysis.
Non-goal: "let AI write the ADR for us".
sessions.exercise: From one ADR, create onboarding summary and a "why is this like this?" answer.

sessions.title: Closing: what changes from Monday?
sessions.goal: Define minimum policy the team can adopt immediately.
points: ADR policy v0: when mandatory, where it lives, ownership.
One thing to start, one thing to stop.
Workshop retro.

closing.title: What you take back on Monday
closing.intro: By the end of the workshop, your team has a shared decision-making operating model where useful ADRs emerge naturally.
points: Decision matrix for choosing options and identifying when formal decisions are needed.
Simple ownership model for preparation, review, and decision authority.
Lifecycle rules for superseding and deprecating ADRs.
Minimum PR and commit integration guidelines.

cta.title: Want your team to reduce misalignment and ship with higher precision?
cta.body: We tailor the workshop to your product and engineering context so your team can make better decisions together under real delivery pressure. ADRs are treated as a useful output of that capability, not as paperwork. Default format is on-site.

consulting.pageTitle: Consulting that helps teams apply workshop principles in real systems
consulting.intro: Training sticks when teams use it on real systems and real decisions. I offer short, focused consulting engagements that build on the same workshop ideas—ADRs, C4, CI/CD and deployment practices, modular design—so your team can apply them where it counts.
audits: Review of your current architecture, deployment setup, and engineering practices, with a focus on what actually blocks delivery or makes change risky.
Identification of delivery bottlenecks and high-leverage improvement areas so you know where to act first.
Written summary and concrete next steps that your teams and leadership can use without requiring ongoing external support.
decisions: Facilitated sessions to capture key technical decisions as ADRs so the rationale outlives the current team.
Support in comparing architecture options against explicit trade-offs so choices are defensible and repeatable.
Help teams articulate risks and constraints so non-technical stakeholders can participate in trade-offs.
mentoring: 1:1 or small-group mentoring for tech leads and senior engineers, aligned with the same concepts as the workshops.
Practical discussions on introducing and sustaining workshop practices—ADRs, boundaries, CI/CD—in your codebase and team.
Feedback on architecture proposals, RFCs, and rollout plans.
consulting.complementsBody: Engagements are short and focused. The aim is knowledge transfer and team autonomy: your people learn to run ADR sessions, maintain diagrams, and improve pipelines themselves. The goal is not ongoing dependency—it's enabling your team to own the practices and decisions after we're done.

about.pageTitle: About Letscode Solutions and my work with teams
about.intro: I work as a senior consultant and trainer focused on helping software engineering teams deliver reliably without losing sight of technical quality.
background: Years of experience working with product development teams and engineering organizations.
Hands-on work across codebases, infrastructure, and delivery pipelines.
Regular speaker at meetups and conferences, with a focus on practical, experience-based talks.
about.approachTitle: How I think about engineering and teams
approach: Practices only matter if they survive everyday pressure. Anything that only works in a slide deck is not interesting.
Architecture should create options, not freeze teams into a specific framework or vendor.
Documentation should be light but reliable: ADRs and diagrams that engineers actually trust.
Good teams learn to make decisions together instead of depending on a single "hero" engineer.
about.credlyBadgeAlt: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner badge on Credly

privacy.intro: This page explains what data processing may happen when you use this website and when external services are loaded.
scopeItems: This notice covers this website and the third-party services integrated in it.
The exact data that each provider receives depends on your interaction and your cookie preferences.
When you follow external links, the destination site handles your data under its own policies.
legalItems: Essential technical processing is based on legitimate interest to operate the site securely.
Optional analytics and marketing scripts are loaded only after your consent in cookie settings.
You can change your choices any time from the footer's Cookie settings link.
privacy.servicesIntro: These providers may receive technical metadata (for example IP address, browser details, and interaction events) when enabled or used.
schedulingItems: Calendly (calendly.com, assets.calendly.com): used on the contact page to book calls.
newsletterItems: AWS Lambda Function URL (lambda-url.eu-central-1.on.aws): used for the training waitlist signup form. Email and first name are sent in a JSON request when you subscribe.
Signup data is processed by a backend workflow operated by Letscode Solutions.
analyticsItems: Google Analytics (googletagmanager.com): page view and usage analytics.
Hotjar (static.hotjar.com): behavior analytics and session insights.
marketingItems: Google tag / Google Ads (googletagmanager.com): ad measurement and remarketing.
Facebook Pixel (connect.facebook.net): marketing and campaign performance measurement.
LinkedIn Insight Tag (snap.licdn.com): campaign and conversion measurement.
externalLinksItems: Sessionize links (sessionize.com): public conference sessions profile.
privacy.cookieControlsBody: You can accept all optional cookies or choose categories (analytics, marketing). Preferences are stored locally so your choice is remembered.
privacy.contactBody: If you have questions about data processing on this site, please use the Contact page.

descriptions.home: Classroom training and consulting for mid–senior software teams: DevOps, architecture, ADRs, C4, CI/CD, and practices that make delivery safer and faster.
descriptions.training: Focused workshops for experienced engineering teams on code review, CI/CD, ADRs, C4 diagrams, and clean architecture—on-site or remote.
descriptions.trainingB2cAds: Conversion-focused two-day Budapest workshop for developers on technical decisions, C4, and ADRs so you can communicate architecture and trade-offs with confidence.
descriptions.trainingB2cTerms: Participation and purchase terms for the Budapest B2C workshop.
descriptions.workshop: Hands-on ADR and requirements alignment workshop for teams who need shared decisions and clearer boundaries.
descriptions.consulting: Consulting to apply architecture and delivery practices in your real systems—reviews, ADR sessions, CI/CD redesign, and guided change.
